concepts.title: Electric Current
concepts.description: Electric current is a flow of electrons. The SI unit of electric current is Ampere (A).

concepts.title: Force on a Moving Charge
concepts.description: The force experienced by a moving charge in a magnetic field is given by F = qvB sinθ, where F is the force, q is the charge, v is the velocity of the charge, B is the magnetic field strength, and θ is the angle between the velocity and the magnetic field.

concepts.title: Motion in a Magnetic Field
concepts.description: A charged particle moving in a magnetic field experiences a force perpendicular to both its direction of motion and the magnetic field direction. The particle will move in a circular path if the magnetic field is uniform.

concepts.title: Right Hand Rule
concepts.description: To determine the direction of the force on a moving charge using the right-hand rule: point your thumb in the direction of the velocity, your fingers in the direction of the magnetic field, and your palm will face the direction of the force.

concepts.title: Electric and Magnetic Fields
concepts.description: Students often confuse electric and magnetic fields. Remember that electric fields exert force on stationary charges, whereas magnetic fields exert force on moving charges.

concepts.title: Magnetic Flux
concepts.description: The SI unit of magnetic flux is the Weber (Wb).

concepts.title: Lenz's Law
concepts.description: Lenz's law states that the direction of the induced current in a conductor is such that it opposes the change in the magnetic flux that produced it.

concepts.title: Biot-Savart Law
concepts.description: The Biot-Savart law describes the magnetic field produced by an electric current. It states that the magnetic field dB due to a small element of a current-carrying wire is proportional to the product of the current, the length of the element, and the sine of the angle between the element and the point where the field is being calculated.
